text: Beautiful music, I appreciate it, it's sung well and appropriately done, very good. I was surprised this morning, I was not included, I'm not going to be here for the apple picking trip. I want you to know that I am an apple connoisseur and an apple snob. I literally eat an apple every day of my life and if you get the opportunity when you go out to the orchard, if they have ambrosia, go pick some ambrosias. If you like crisp, sweet apples, look for Envy and go buy or get some Envy apples. Now if you want to know more about apples, see me after the service, okay? I honestly, I just finished a book on Johnny Appleseed and there really was a Johnny Appleseed and he was a great man and his vision was that some of the settlers going over the mountains, the pioneers, were so poor, so impoverished just to get to where they were going that they would have no good food supply or it would be difficult for them to do. He literally planted orchards all through the Appalachians, the Alleghenies, the Blue Ridge, so that as they went, they could take twigs and branches and then replant and his vision was if they did that everywhere this side of the Mississippi, at least people would have some good fruit and some good food for their life. So there are and were some good people with a good vision to be kind and helpful to others. Alright, that's the nicest I'm going to be all morning, alright? I'm going to preach now. It is an absolute privilege and honor to be here. Pastor, thank you for this opportunity and invitation. Thank you for being here and I'm looking forward to what God is going to do in our midst. I'm asking God to speak to me, help me as much as I'm asking for God to speak to you and help you. Having said all that, Psalm 31 this morning, Psalm 31. And for our text, though we'll look at other verses, we're going to begin reading in verse 14, Psalm 31 and verse 14. And there the Bible says this, David speaking under the inspiration of the Spirit of God, But I trusted in Thee, O Lord, I said, Thou art my God. My times are in Thy hand. Deliver me from the hand of mine enemies, and from them that persecute me. Make Thy face to shine upon Thy servant, and save me for Thy mercy's sake. Let's pray. Father, we're very grateful for the opportunity to be together this morning. We thank You for Your Holy Spirit. I pray that He this morning would take Your Word and speak to our hearts through it. Lord, we're gathered here because we know we need to hear from You. And I pray that You would accomplish that. I ask You to encourage people. I pray that You would instruct people. But I also pray that You would convict and challenge us. And Lord, where You see something in our life that needs to be strengthened, changed, would You convict us about that and then help us to accomplish what You want. I pray especially for those that may be here this morning in our presence that have never trusted Christ as their Savior. God, would You expose to them their great need. Beyond their need, would You reveal to them how much You love them and how much You desire to save them. So Lord, work in all of our hearts. Help us, we pray. In Jesus' name, amen. Here's my question to begin the message. In verse 14, David says, but I trusted in Thee. Why is David saying he's in a place where he has to bring attention to the fact that he's trusting in the Lord? Well the answer is in verse 9. Have mercy upon me, O Lord, for I am in trouble. The trouble that David is in and the word that he uses is not indicative that it's a difficult moment but it'll pass. No, he is in real trouble. We're not sure if this is from the time of his life when Saul was chasing him. We suspect and I agree that it is probably written around the time where Absalom has rebelled and David is being cast out of his palace, cast out of his city. Deceitfully, Absalom has asked leave, to go to leave the city. He's gone to Hebron, taken Hebron, taken that fortified city, raised an army and is now marching back towards Jerusalem. There are people that are making decisions that are certainly terrible decisions for David. They're forsaking him. They fear that Absalom's army is stronger than anything David has. David is aging and David is feeling forsaken. Notice what he says in verse 10. For my life is spent with grief, my years with sighing, my strength faileth because of mine iniquity, all my bones are consumed. I was a reproach among all my enemies but especially among my neighbors. Now I read that just so that we understand this is a deep moment of trouble in David's life and he is really concerned about what is going to happen to him, happen to his family. It is a serious moment into which he interjects this, but I trusted God. Why did you trust God David? Because my times are in His hands. David realized what you and I realized, that one of the realities of our lives in this world is that we experience seasons of stress. We experience seasons of trouble. We're surrounded by difficult moments, difficult times, problems if you will. In our nation today, who cannot realize the difficulty of the moment we're in, the social unrest. Not only is there social unrest, political unrest, there's economic uncertainty. Who knows what interest rates are going to do? Who knows what this is going to have, what this bubble is going to bust? It's always a question of what's going to happen. We've lived through a pandemic, now we're back into seasons of war. There's just always this possibility of a season of stress. Have I encouraged you yet? We'll get to it. That's why David said, man, we live in this ever-changing sea. We understand that Jesus always knew where we were and always knew what we needed. We're not the first generation to face stress and trouble. He had a kingdom to manage. He was surrounded all of his days as king with wars and fighting. He had a very difficult family situation, some of his own making. But my point is this, that in life, if you and I think that it is always a fairy tale, it is not so. And I want you to know that though we are not fighting giants and killing bears and lions and we're not kings of kingdoms, we are people who live on God's earth and we do face the seasons of difficulty in our lives. lives and one of the great things that you and I must come to understand is that we can trust that God is our God and our seasons, our lives are lived in God's hands. Notice those two things with me today. Number one, my times. David's life and our lives are definitely marked by change and instability. That word that David used for times is a word that indicates seasons or changeableness. I'll speak more about that in a moment. But I just want you to recognize this morning that our lives are lived in seasons. Let me try to illustrate it. Several years ago, I was born an infant, more than several. And in infancy, I was completely helpless. If my mother and father had not provided for me, had there not been a gracious God, I couldn't have survived. But you know what? I grew out of that age of infancy and I became a toddler. And I was such a precious toddler. You know the season of being a toddler. It's a challenging season. But you know what? We grow out of being toddlers and we become juniors. And as juniors, the world is exciting and life is exciting and mom and dad are great and it's a wonderful season of life. But we grow out of being juniors and we become teenagers. And we then understand that it's our job to teach mother and dad how they're to live and how life is supposed to be. But we grow out of being teenagers. And then those truly wonderful years come normally where we find that one that God has made for us and we're going to share a life with them. And then we grow out of that season of newlyweds to become young parents. And it's a challenging season, but it's also one of the greatest seasons of life as we see those children born into our homes. And then that season comes of being an empty nester and we don't know what we're going to do, how we're going to survive. And then we find out it's a pretty good deal. And then after being empty nesters, we become seniors and we're almost back to infancy. Think about it. You have a point preacher? Yeah. Yeah. We live our lives in seasons. And they come and they go. They stay for a while and then we move on to something else. And some of them are incredibly precious and some of them are incredibly difficult. David one day was sitting on a hillside tending sheep, creating wonderful psalms and hymns about the Lord and creation. But the next moment in life, he's called down to the house. He stands before Samuel, the judge, the prophet, and he's anointed king. One moment he's a hero with his foot on Goliath's chest, but the next he's dodging a spear from King Saul. The next moment he's a king in Hebron and then Jerusalem and living in a palace. But the next he's an outcast being chased by his own son. I'm simply saying that we don't know what tomorrow holds and we're not guaranteed that tomorrow is a sweet season. Here's the truth, we're going to pass through some wonderful times, but we're also going to face some truly difficult times in our lives. One of my favorite things as a pastor, preacher, was when a young couple would have a child. And we had a very young congregation and I was often, I did way more weddings than funerals. And I remember going to the hospital one time and this young couple, he was a Marine and his wife, they had just had their first child. And he and I were standing off to the side and she was over in a rocking chair in a little blue blanket and a little blue beanie, you know, and she was rocking her new little boy. And the husband and I, we were talking about, I'm quite sure, serious things, maybe football. And I overheard the wife and she was so softly, she could have cared less about us. Her attention was that little baby. And I heard her say something like this, I don't ever want this moment to pass. I just always want you to be in my arms and my little baby and my little boy. And I overheard her say that and down inside me as a fundamental Baptist preacher, I wanted to turn to her and say, well sister, that baby's going to grow up. It's not always going to be like this. They're going to grow up and they're going to love somebody else more than you and leave you. Aren't you glad I fought the urge to say that? But that was the truth. Because life's always moving. It's always moving. It never stays the same. And sometimes we just want to reach out to the Lord and say, leave it here. Stop moving it. Stop letting the world turn. I remember my grandma used to watch her show. It was a soap opera called As the World Turns and it followed the lives of these people and they got old and they had problems and they had good days and bad days. And I began to recognize it was not something you might want to watch, but it was mighty true in what it said. The world keeps turning and we never know what tomorrow brings. But sometimes what tomorrow brings is not easy and not pleasant, but it's part of what God is allowing and doing in our lives. I am so thankful that in those seasons that are moving and ever-changing, mine are lived in the hands of God and so are yours. Think about that with me for a moment. David's times may have been unstable. David's life may have been ever-changing, but David's God was stable and unchanging. Think of words that he used. He described God as a rock, as a fortress, as a house of defense. In other words, man, everything is swirling about me, but God never moves. God never changes. One of the attributes of God that is so precious to me is that God is immutable. We often talk about God being omniscient and it's a wonderful truth that God knows everything. God knows everything about me and God knows everything I need. God is omnipresent. I am so thankful God is present with us this morning. My wife in Charlotte, I'm glad God is present with my wife and with my son as he stands to preach this morning. I'm glad that God is present with every missionary. I'm glad God is present with my kids and grandkids scattered across the country. God is ever-present. God is omnipotent. There's never anything that we face that God cannot care for, God cannot take control of. God is omnipotent. All things are possible for God. And we know those omnis, but sometimes we're not as careful to appreciate that God is immutable. And as everything swirls around us, God is the same that he's always been. And God's truth is the same as it's always been. His mercy endures forever. He is exactly today what he was yesterday and tomorrow when my life changes, he will exactly be what he was today. He never changes. Now let me mention two aspects of that that's important for us. God is not only immutable, God is sovereign. Sometimes we're a little bit afraid of that word sovereign. It's a wonderful word and a wonderful truth. The word David uses for times is the same word that Daniel uses, and I won't have you turn there for the sake of time. But it's the same word that Daniel uses when he says this, Daniel answered and said, blessed be the name of God forever and ever, for wisdom and might are his. And he changeth the times and the seasons. He removeth kings and setteth up kings. He giveth wisdom unto the wise and knowledge to them that know understanding. Our world seems to be utterly chaotic. But I want you to understand that in the midst of our chaos as we see our world, God is absolutely in control. Nothing takes God by surprise. God is not sitting on the edge of heaven hoping it works out. God knows exactly where he is taking planet earth. And by the way, God knows exactly where he is taking you and I. God is God in the future. God is God in the past. God is God in the present. And God is sovereign over all men. God knows who he is going to raise up and allow to rule. But God knows when he's going to take them down and let someone else rule. This world is not moving by the manipulative hand of man. It is moving by the settled story of God. And sometimes we get so frustrated and we get maybe so frightened because of the moment, but you and I must understand that our times are in God's hands. And he is a sovereign God moving history his direction. But let me also say this, God is also a personal God. Aren't you glad of that? God is not just some foreboding force. God is not just some unknowable power. God is personal. I love what David says in verse 14. Thou art my God. I am so thankful this morning that I can say God is my God. I am his son. I did not get saved as a child. I wasn't raised in a Christian home. I have wonderful parents. Please don't hear me saying that they were not good people. They were wonderful people. But I am grateful for this, that I know the dividing line. I know how God spoke to me. I know what my need was and I called out to him to save me and it's all been different because thou art my God. What a joy. He is a personal God. You see, God is both transcendent and imminent. What do you mean by that, preacher? I mean that God is big enough, so big, that he rules the universe. But he is so personal that he met with me in that wonderful apartment I have that you have provided.
